title: Salary to Hourly Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Calculate your hourly rate from any annual salary. Estimate daily, weekly, biweekly, and monthly pay. Standard 2,080-hour work year (40 hrs × 52 weeks).
intro: **Turn your annual salary into an hourly rate in seconds.** Whether you're comparing a job offer to a freelance contract, negotiating pay, or budgeting by the hour, this calculator breaks down your compensation into every time unit that matters — hourly, daily, weekly, biweekly, and monthly. Adjust hours per week and weeks worked per year to match your actual schedule.
keyTakeaway: A $75,000/yr salary at 40 hrs/week over 52 weeks equals $36.06/hr — or $288.46/day and $6,250/month before taxes.
useCases: Compare a salaried job offer against a freelance hourly rate
Calculate your effective hourly wage when you work more than 40 hrs/week
Estimate pay for a part-time or seasonal position (fewer weeks worked)
Set a minimum freelance billing rate based on your target annual income
Verify a contractor's quoted hourly rate against an equivalent salary
Budget monthly and biweekly cash flow from an annual compensation package
explanation:
## How It Works

This calculator converts an annual salary into equivalent pay rates across different time periods using a single core formula.

## Formula

```
Total Hours/Year = Hours per Week × Weeks per Year
Hourly Rate     = Annual Salary ÷ Total Hours/Year
Daily Rate      = Hourly Rate × 8
Weekly Pay      = Hourly Rate × Hours per Week
Biweekly Pay    = Weekly Pay × 2
Monthly Pay     = Annual Salary ÷ 12
```

The **standard full-time work year** in the United States is **2,080 hours** (40 hours/week × 52 weeks). This is the figure used by the U.S. Office of Personnel Management (OPM) for federal pay calculations.

## Worked Example

| Input | Value |
|---|---|
| Annual Salary | $75,000 |
| Hours per Week | 40 |
| Weeks per Year | 52 |

| Output | Calculation | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Total Hours | 40 × 52 | 2,080 hrs |
| Hourly Rate | $75,000 ÷ 2,080 | **$36.06/hr** |
| Daily Rate | $36.06 × 8 | **$288.46/day** |
| Weekly Pay | $36.06 × 40 | **$1,442.31/wk** |
| Biweekly Pay | $1,442.31 × 2 | **$2,884.62** |
| Monthly Pay | $75,000 ÷ 12 | **$6,250.00/mo** |

## Adjusting for Real-World Schedules

- **Part-time or seasonal work:** Reduce weeks per year (e.g., 48 weeks if you take 4 weeks unpaid leave). This raises your effective hourly cost to an employer.
- **Overtime-heavy roles:** If you regularly work 50 hrs/week, set hours to 50. Your true hourly equivalent drops — a $75,000 salary at 50 hrs/week = $28.85/hr, not $36.06.
- **Freelance rate buffer:** Freelancers should target 25–35% above their equivalent salaried hourly rate to cover self-employment taxes (~15.3%), benefits, and unpaid downtime.

## When NOT to Apply This Formula

- **Gross vs. net pay:** All results are **before taxes**. Federal, state, and FICA deductions will reduce your take-home significantly.
- **Salaried exempt employees:** If you work 55 hrs/week but are paid a flat salary, your legal overtime protections may differ under the FLSA.
- **Paid time off (PTO):** If your employer pays for vacation and sick days, your *effective* hourly rate is higher than this formula shows, since you're paid for hours not worked.

faq.q: What is the standard number of work hours in a year?
faq.a: The U.S. standard is 2,080 hours per year, based on 40 hours per week × 52 weeks. The U.S. Office of Personnel Management uses this figure for federal civilian pay rates. Some employers use 2,087 hours for more precise annual calculations.

faq.q: Should I include vacation weeks when calculating my hourly rate?
faq.a: If your employer pays you during vacation (PTO), keep weeks at 52 — you're paid for those weeks even without working. If your time off is unpaid, subtract those weeks. For example, 2 weeks unpaid = 50 weeks, which raises your hourly rate since the same salary covers fewer hours.

faq.q: How do I use this to set my freelance rate?
faq.a: Find your equivalent salaried hourly rate, then add 25–40% to cover self-employment tax (15.3%), health insurance, retirement contributions, and unbillable hours. A $36.06 salaried equivalent should translate to at least $45–$50/hr as a freelancer to break even.

faq.q: Why does my daily rate use 8 hours instead of my actual hours per day?
faq.a: The daily rate defaults to 8 hours, the conventional business day. If you work 10-hour days, multiply your hourly rate by 10 to get your actual daily earnings. The formula always uses your entered hours per week for weekly, biweekly, and hourly calculations.

faq.q: Does this calculator account for taxes?
faq.a: No. All outputs are gross (pre-tax) figures. After federal income tax, state tax, and FICA (Social Security + Medicare, 7.65% employee share), your net take-home will be meaningfully lower. Use a dedicated paycheck calculator for after-tax estimates.

faq.q: What's the difference between biweekly and semimonthly pay?
faq.a: Biweekly means 26 paychecks per year (every 2 weeks). Semimonthly means 24 paychecks per year (twice per month). This calculator shows biweekly pay (weekly × 2). Semimonthly pay = annual salary ÷ 24.

faq.q: How does this help when comparing a job offer to my current salary?
faq.a: Convert both salaries to hourly rates using their actual expected hours. A $80,000 job requiring 50 hrs/week ($30.77/hr) pays less per hour than a $70,000 job at 40 hrs/week ($33.65/hr). Hourly comparison reveals the true trade-off.

faq.q: What if I work part-time, say 20 hours per week?
faq.a: Enter 20 in the hours per week field. Total annual hours drop to 1,040, so your hourly rate doubles compared to a 40-hr week at the same salary. A $37,500 part-time salary at 20 hrs/week still yields $36.06/hr — the same rate as $75,000 full-time.

faq.q: Is monthly pay calculated as annual ÷ 12 or weekly × 4?
faq.a: This calculator uses annual salary ÷ 12, which is the standard payroll method. Multiplying weekly pay by 4 gives only 48 weeks of pay per year and underestimates monthly income by about 8.3%.

sources.name: U.S. Office of Personnel Management — Fact Sheet: Hours of Work
BLS Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics (OEWS)

dataUpdate.notes: Core formula is mathematically stable and does not depend on annually changing rates. Update self-employment tax rate note if IRS SE tax rate changes. Verify OPM standard hours reference annually.
dataUpdate.source: U.S. Office of Personnel Management — Fact Sheet: Hours of Work
